Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 3 - MySQL Tables: the SQL Commands You Actually Need

Last time we covered the basics of working with databases. But inside a database there's another thing called a table, and a table is where your data actually lives. You can picture it like a spreadsheet or a notebook. So this time we're going to learn the basic SQL commands for working with tables.

Before we get into table syntax, we need to get familiar with the common column data types you'll run into. Here are the ones I personally reach for most often (this is just my own short list, not an exhaustive one):
is a fixed-length string. If your string length is going to vary, I'd go with
Most commonly used for things like message boards. Besides
Whole numbers, meaning no decimal point.
, which is better suited for smaller numeric values, but honestly I just use
takes up twice the space because it carries higher precision. For most cases
If you don't explicitly set a time, it'll automatically record the current time for you.

Again, that's just the list of types I've used and use often. If you want to dig deeper into all the different data types, I'd recommend this article from a fellow developer for a much more detailed breakdown ->
In the previous post we already created a database called
. Earlier we used a bank as our analogy, and we'll keep that going here.

Generally a bank has a bunch of little safe deposit boxes. If you don't plan things out, it's just an iron box and nothing more. So to properly organize the data we're going to store later, we need to add a table to hold it. Say we want to build a table recording the height and weight of students in a class. We'd use the following syntax to create the table:

CREATE TABLE hw_table (
  id int(10) NOT NULL auto_increment,
  name varchar(5) NOT NULL default '',
  weight int(5) NOT NULL default 0,
  height float(5) NOT NULL default 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (id)
) AUTO_INCREMENT=1;
When you see some unfamiliar stuff, don't panic, because I'm about to explain it. First let's get to know a few of the English keywords above ↓
- the default value, with the default data or value coming right after.
- sets the index, in other words the

CREATE TABLE  'table_name' (
  column_name_1 data_type column_options
  column_name_2 data_type column_options
  ...
  index column
)
But there's one thing to watch out for here. When you run the SQL above, make sure you're actually inside
, otherwise the table might get created somewhere else or fail to be created at all!
Once it runs successfully, you'll see a new table appear inside
Tables have a query syntax similar to the one we used to list databases. It's mainly for pulling info on a specific table's columns. Let's try inspecting the columns of the
We've created a table and inspected its columns, so naturally there's a way to update a column too. Say we want to change the
data type. The syntax would look like this:
ALTER TABLE hw_table CHANGE COLUMN weight weight float;
syntax we just learned to check whether the column got changed successfully ↓
Same idea: if we want to rename
, we just adapt the syntax above to get what we want:
ALTER TABLE hw_table CHANGE COLUMN weight itweight int;
And of course the result is exactly what we expected ↓
Last up is deleting a column. Say we want to remove the

column we just modified. That's where the delete-column syntax comes in. But you'll notice the delete syntax is really similar to the update syntax, so be careful when you type it (one typo and it's game over):
ALTER TABLE hw_table DROP COLUMN itweight;
to check the result. Normally when you run this in phpMyAdmin, it pops up a confirmation message ↓
Confirmation message (ignore the lowercase command in the image)
I mentioned the reason earlier: deleting is a genuinely dangerous action, which is why it asks you to confirm "Do you really want to delete this column?" ↓
Deleted successfully (ignore the lowercase command in the image)

You might notice I put the add-column syntax later on. That's mainly to show that after creating a table, you can smoothly update a column → inspect columns and delete a column. So now it's time for the add-column syntax. You'll see it's also really similar to the update-column syntax, so I'll skip the chatter and go straight to the code. Let's restore the
ALTER TABLE hw_table ADD COLUMN weight int;
And just like that, we've successfully added the deleted column back~
Same old warning: this command is dangerous, so please be careful with it. I won't demo it here to avoid messing up the operations in later chapters, so I'll just give you the command structure~

This one is similar to deleting, but it mainly clears out all the data inside a table. Since our table doesn't have any data in it yet, running it won't cause any problems. After you run it, it keeps the table's structure and columns. In short, only the data inside disappears:
Finally, let's round up all the SQL commands covered in this post.
CREATE TABLE 'table_name' (   column_name_1 data_type column_options   column_name_2 data_type column_options   ...   index column )

Last time we covered the basics of working with databases, but inside a database there's another thing called a table, and a table is where your data actually lives. You can picture it like a spreadsheet or a notebook. So this time we're going to learn the basic SQL commands for working with tables - creating them, inspecting columns, updating, deleting, adding columns, and wiping a table clean.
